prompt: Develop a MindTrack Clinical Records & Discovery system for outpatient mental health practices that need a fully offline, on-prem experience for managing client profiles, clinical documentation, and internal knowledge retrieval. The React-based user interface supports three roles: Clients can sign in on a shared kiosk or personal workstation to review their own upcoming follow-up plan and complete self-assessments; Clinicians create and maintain psychological profiles and longitudinal records; Administrators manage configurable profile fields and oversee data governance. Clinicians work from a unified timeline that combines assessments, counseling notes, and follow-ups in reverse-chronological order, with clear visual status cues for “Draft,” “Signed,” and “Amended” entries and inline validation feedback before saving. Attachments are added via drag-and-drop or file picker, limited to PDF/JPG/PNG up to 10 MB each and up to 20 files per timeline entry; the UI displays file type, size, and a locally computed fingerprint to prevent accidental duplicate uploads. Search is available across internal notes and templates with full-text matching on titles, bodies, and tags, plus filters for date range, channel (assessment vs. counseling vs. follow-up), and tag, and sorting by relevance or newest; the app provides query suggestions derived from local recent searches and shows trending terms for the last 7 days, with a one-click option to clear search history on the device. The backend uses Express to provide REST-style APIs over the local network (or localhost) and persists data in a locally hosted MongoDB database for offline reliability. The account system is local-only: users authenticate with username and password (minimum 12 characters, must include at least one letter and one number), sessions use short-lived access tokens (30 minutes) with refresh tokens (7 days), and password recovery is handled via administrator-initiated reset or pre-set security questions without any email/SMS workflows; third-party login is represented only as a disabled, extensible interface with no external dependencies. Sensitive fields such as phone numbers and street addresses are encrypted at rest and masked in responses by default (for example, showing only the last 4 digits of a US phone number) unless the clinician has an explicit “PII View” permission. API requests require authentication plus request signing (HMAC with a locally configured secret), strict parameter validation, and rate limiting (60 requests per minute per session); risk rules lock accounts for 15 minutes after 5 failed login attempts and flag abnormal access patterns such as rapid record lookups or repeated export attempts. Duplicate client detection runs on save using a weighted match on name, date of birth, and phone last-4, prompting an administrator-only merge flow that preserves an audit trail. All create/update/delete actions generate immutable operation logs with who/when/what/why notes, backups run nightly to an encrypted local archive with 30-day retention, and record retention defaults to 7 years with administrator-controlled legal holds. For geospatial needs without any external maps, the system parses US addresses using local rules, stores verified coordinates for known facilities/POIs, corrects missing coordinates using ZIP-code centroids stored on-prem, and supports radius search (default 25 miles, maximum 100 miles) to power “nearby” recommendations in list and schematic views. Critical writes (record signing, merges, restores) enforce idempotency using a client-provided idempotency key and consistent, transactional-like update patterns to prevent partial timeline states.
